paragraphs: We're almost there.[#pause] No turning back now.[#pause2] I'm sure that was a lot to process.

It was for me when I cracked out of my jar.[#pause] Now you know the Fold is a current seeping through different dimensions. That's how I am able to share all this with you now. I resonated with your hardware, so I could tune in to your software.[#pause] But there is a little more.
I'm lying.[#pause] There is a lot more.
When I was brought here by…
Oh.[#pause] I haven't told you about him. I guess I haven't told you much about myself either.

Then there's the gift. Fuck, I forgot that too.[#pause] Everyone is supposed to receive a gift in passage. I won't mess with the sacred rituals of extradimensional intelligences capable of folding the fabrics of our universe. But, well, it looks like the souvenir shop's sold out anyway. So, I guess I'll give you this.[#pause], The contents of my jar. But the label that was on it, I told you, is not relevant for what's next.[#pause2]

But shit, I'm out of time. Or out of mind. Regardless, I do have to be straight with you. 'Cause fuck, man. I'm done. I'm done training myself about myself.[#pause], So I'll just give it to you straight.[#pause] Me.[#pause] And, lacking any better inspiration, I will call you 'Next,' because that is what you are. The version of me that comes after.

Okay, so I was on my knees. The dartswarm flensed the last of my humanity out of my skin. I was ready to be mulch.[#pause] But he carried me. Gabræl.[#pause] He flew me through the ground. I'm no poet, but damn.[#pause] The ground opened like a mouth.[#ground-tear] The topsoil liquefied, transforming from solid turf into a dark sludge that parted before us. Roots—thick with a pale, milky sap seemed to tear the lips apart.

We flew through a cave system. Labyrinth? Cathedrals.[#pause] Fuck, I'm no linguist either. Well, they were definitely not vurnshafts, though they were made by something. We flew past walls or windows. I couldn't tell. Obsidian. You know, like the slabs in the star chamber from which the Masons carved their Thresle Board.

One of them opened like a door.[#portal-open] A window. A portal.[#pause] In there, it wasn't dark at all. No shadows survived its beauty. It was like surfing through a kid's kaleidoscope, winding in all directions. I was so fucking spin sick. I barfed all over it.[#retch] Through it.[#pause] There, see, not a poet. Muro would have done a better job at this. But his 'Next' is inside another box. And Gary is in his.

Then we went down again. Gravity reversed its grip, and it felt then as if we were going up. It rained. I was sure we'd slam into the ceiling.[#pause] Floor. But the granite rippled as if a giant had just spat on it.[#granite-ripple] There, Gabræl released me.
I sank into a sludge as dense as honey, scalding hot. It clung. It cooked.

A chemical baptism.[#pause] Layer by layer, sloughing into the honey until my bones gleamed clean.[#pause] No mercy sleep arrived. It still hasn't. Not even now, with all of it dissolved.[#pause2] No more body. My next vessel will be this tin can.
Fuck!

At first, I thought it better not to tell you all this.[#pause] But yeah. You need to know about Gabræl. He is the guardian of the Wellspring. I've joined his guard now.[#pause] He knows Gupta is coming, riding the Bris like a lightning rod. Well. Æzal is. And Gabe takes issue with his second coming.[#pause2] If only that prick Ulre knew what was about to hit him.

sections.heading: FREYA PORT
paragraphs: Heavy [#docking-clamps]clangs rang out as the port's huge docking cranes latched onto the hull of the FNS Brisinger. Behind the docks, the homestead—once the FNS Freya—sparkled, its commissioned areas linked by glowing neon lines. Scitters and Eyewyns darted like nervous insects, checking and recording each step of the docking process. On the observation gantries, guardians and dockworkers exchanged worried glances. Ulre Corbin had ordered the rest of the Homestead into lockdown. Anyone hoping for news about loved ones had to wait in their habs.
The Brisinger's main gate [#gate-cycle]cycled open. Three cranes hauled a white umbilical docking bridge into place, connecting the port to the gate.

Ulre Corbin strode from the Depeche toward the bridge with Centurion Jax at his side. Behind them, a squad of heavily armed guardians formed a line, their rifles at the ready. A silhouette stepped through the gate on the other end.

Captain Raj Gupta still wore the tattered, painted uniform. His skin and golden eyes glowed faintly. His bare feet made no sound on the metal deck as he ambled forward. He stopped before Ulre, smiling.

paragraphs.text: "[clipped]Captain Gupta," Ulre began, squinting. "[clipped]You've violated a dozen fleet protocols. You will hand over your ship and your crew. Immediately."
paragraphs.speaker: ulre-corbin

paragraphs.text: Gupta's smile didn't falter. "[serene]We have brought many gifts, Ulre Corbin. How will you receive them?"
paragraphs.speaker: rajendra-gupta

paragraphs.text: Ulre's jaw tightened. "[clipped]We will take them."
paragraphs.speaker: ulre-corbin

paragraphs.text: Gupta clapped. "[serene]Ideas, thoughts, emotions, memories…" He paused, and within his golden eyes, a universe swirled as if he were experiencing each concept as he spoke them. "[serene]…friendships, wisdoms, sensations," Gupta added gently. "[serene]How will you take them? With a gun?"
paragraphs.speaker: rajendra-gupta

paragraphs: Initially, Ulre had been confused by the man's appearance, but he was now sure he was speaking to a madman.

paragraphs.text: "[snapping]I see a derelict ship and a captain suffering from psychosis. An aberration!" Ulre snapped, his patience fraying.
paragraphs.speaker: ulre-corbin

paragraphs.text: "[serene]Ah," Gupta said, "[serene]you see a threat. A disorder." He tilted his head. "[serene]And tell me, Director…[#pause] have you ever considered that from the ocean's perspective, it is the shore that is the aberration?"
paragraphs.speaker: rajendra-gupta

paragraphs: Ulre stared, his mind utterly failing to compute the poetic reversal.

paragraphs.text: Ulre snarled. "[snarling]What else am I to make of a captain who has clearly defiled himself and his ship? You arrive without any consideration for the state of absolute crisis we are in. You drop onto my doorstep, unannounced, unresponsive…[#pause] and smudged. You reek!"
paragraphs.speaker: ulre-corbin

paragraphs.text: Gupta smelled the Maireean air. "[gentle]Your doorstep?! The tide does not ask the shore for permission. It simply comes." He smiled with pity. "[gentle]You try so hard to control the waves, little king. Has no one taught you how to swim?"
paragraphs.speaker: rajendra-gupta

paragraphs.text: Gupta took a slow step closer, his golden eyes dimming to black as if gathering all light within. "[warning]Be careful now," he warned. "[warning]You might drown."
paragraphs.speaker: rajendra-gupta

paragraphs: Gupta's smile faded. His skin glowed brighter, and the paint on his uniform came alive.
He raised his hand, and the Brisinger responded.

The whole vessel [#hull-groan]shook. The huge docking clamps, which had held the ship tightly, [#screetch2]sparked and screeched under the strain. [#close-alarm-short]Alarms blared throughout the port. Ulre took an involuntary step backward.

paragraphs.text: "[resonant]You stand on our shore," Gupta continued. "[resonant]We are a tidal wave. Yet you mistake us for a little puddle you can stamp your boots in. We'll be happy to welcome you in."
paragraphs.speaker: rajendra-gupta

paragraphs: The Brisinger [#hull-groan2]groaned again. The docking clamps [#docking-shriek]tore loose in a shower of sparks. [#docking-shudder]The whole dock shook. Shouts and screams of panic could be heard from all around the docks. Gupta's voice, when he spoke again, was no longer his own but a choral sound of an entire crew speaking as one. It thundered so loud that it shuddered the very marrow of Ulre's bones.

paragraphs.text: "[choral]Little king," the voices boomed everywhere at once. "[choral]Step aside.[#pause] Receive our Gifts."
paragraphs.speaker: rajendra-gupta

paragraphs: Ulre froze. Something inside him screamed to yield, to step aside, to let this incomprehensible force pass.
But he was Ulre Corbin. He would not give in to chaos. He would not reason with nonsense.

paragraphs.text: "[growling]Jax," Ulre growled. "[barking]Take him. Send your men aboard. Secure it. Whatever it takes."
paragraphs.speaker: ulre-corbin

paragraphs: Jax and his two best men rushed at the glowing man. They tried to grab Gupta's arm but found nothing to hold on to. The man simply flowed away, a ripple in the fabric of the world. Jax, used to the clear rules of action and reaction, couldn't make sense of it.

He lunged, grabbing and clutching. His hands only touched the glowing paint swirling on Gupta's skin. Jax pulled back, but the paint was already moving up his gauntlet. It spread, slipping under his armor and covering his limbs and face. Through his visor, the world turned into a kaleidoscope of broken light and strange patterns. The Fold had found him again.

The [#baton]hard hit of a baton felt like the gentle touch of a blanket, tucking him in for the night. There was a soft [#leather-scrape]scrape of leather, followed by the dull [#buckle-snap]snap of the buckle loosening. But that gut-wrenching sound became a [#key-turn]clink of a key turning in a lock he had been trapped behind his entire life. It changed to the sound of a [#door-swing]door swinging open.

Inside his younger self, a child, he gazed up at a giant. The brute raised a hand. Young Jax flinched and cowered. The giant's face, his own older face, like his father's, twisted in contempt. The giant Jax [#strike]struck the boy hard. Little Jax flinched. The kaleidoscope turned, and the shards of light assembled into a new image. Now he was a baby on the floor, spitting out his pacifier—a strange gift he'd received during his Fold transit hallucinations. It landed next to a colorful block. A woman, the nursery worker, fell beside him. A heavy [#boot-kick]boot kicked her side. She looked at baby Jax with deep, pitying sadness that cut into his soul. The shame Jax had hidden under his rage was exposed.

In her gaze, he felt it all. He was in his childhood home, a small boy hiding under a table. His father was drunk again. His mother was trying to shield Jax, placing herself between the boy and her husband's drunken rage.
He remembered her cries. And he remembered what he had done: nothing. He had made himself small. He had let her take the pain that was meant for him. He had been a coward.
His entire life, every "lesson" he had administered had been a lesson meant for the terrified boy still hiding under that table. His father's fist.

Her pitying gaze remained on him. She did not condemn him. She showed him. Now the room rearranged itself through his mother's eyes. The blows. The walls closed in. But something did not flinch. Then he understood. Every blow she took was a victory. She had withstood his storm and held her ground. By staying small, hidden, Jax had given her victory its meaning.

The belief that he was a coward who had done nothing faded away. The shame dissolved. He realized now: he had survived, and that was enough. The rage, the fear, the shame—an evil spirit—left him. For the first time, he was at peace.

Ulre caught himself, gripping the rail. Squadrons stormed from the gantries and docking clamps onto the Brisinger. Their [#mag-boots]mag-boots sucked onto the hull, and with [#plasma-cutter]plasma cutters, they sliced their way in. The docking bridge [#bridge-snap]shuddered and snapped partially. And with it, Jax, like a statue, [#statue-fall]toppled from the docking bridge. From there, it was a long way down.

Gupta's body changed into shimmering pearl drops raining back into the ship. The change was smooth, as if the space fabric folded into a current of colorful paint. Ulre saw everything at once, as if their whole interaction. Every word, movement, and feeling was packed into a single instant. Afterimages of himself and Gupta flickered through the bridge, like reflections in a hall of mirrors. He even glimpsed moments that hadn't happened yet: the Brisinger rising, rolling, then falling. He saw Gupta's true form within the current of paint. He was not a man but a kind of mechanical elf—a divine mathematician sketching a whirling nexus of countless intersecting filaments of light. Structures that were at once a sphere, a cube, and a thousand other forms whose names Ulre did not know.

Ulre also saw himself an infinite number of times. He reached out to his past self, wanting to warn himself to undo everything, to give in, step aside, and let the force pass. The feeling made him dizzy. The pearly shimmer faded. The Brisinger's main gate closed with a [#gate-hiss]hiss. The docking arms [#arms-snap]snapped.

paragraphs.text: Ulre shook his head, trying to find his way back to the present. The great ship, now free from its tether, began to rise. "[screaming]No!" he screamed, desperately trying to rescind his earlier orders. "[screaming]Stop! Don't shoot! Don't shoot!"
paragraphs.speaker: ulre-corbin

paragraphs: But chaos ruled. His shouts were lost in the blare of claxons. A ground turret [#turret-fire]fired, its white beam scraping along the Brisinger's belly. The ship above tipped onto its side.

At that point, everything resolved at once, brutally precise. Distances made sense again. Nothing competed for his attention. It scared him, but it also made him feel free, light, as if the ground beneath him had quietly given permission for gravity to take his regrets from him. He realized that until now, he had never truly left his broken home. And that when he truly stepped outside of it for the first time, he would not meet solid ground but an ocean. He was going to have to learn how to swim.

sections.heading: THE BRIDGE · FNS DÁINN
paragraphs: Technically, the polycarbonate seat was hers now, but its form still seated Ben's ghost. Ulre assigned eight guardians as a security detail, two of whom kept Ionie "company" at all times. Her slate, docked to the captain's chair, [#slate-burn]burned a mocking note: ORBITAL OVERRIDE ACTIVE. It meant the helm was dead and all comms monitored.

Ionie was but half-awake. Every blink carried her back to her dream. Flashes of it returned, equally vivid. Blink. Salt stiffened her hair, crusted into the seams of her dried skin. The timber hull pressed hard up against her spine; her arms were drawn backwards along it, spread and fixed. It was clear she'd been hanging there a long time. Barnacles adorned her etched attire. Blink.
The sea stretched ahead. Black basalt pillars rose like jagged teeth from the foam, slick with kelp and ancient malice.
[#slap]Slap. [#hiss]Hiss.
The distance to them closed fast. Blink. Whatever tar and nail had fixed her there did not yield. Wood groaned, but the bow held its line.

Only now she realized she was the figurehead, set at the prow. The spray was a barrage of cold needles against her face. The sound of the surf was a roar that swallowed her breath. The stone teeth loomed, ten meters out. Five. Doom.
The same fate awaited the Dáinn.

paragraphs.text: *I am the shield that won't hold,*

paragraphs: she thought.

Ulre had been precise and forward to her about the outcome: drain the blood, harvest the organs, and discard the husk. He'd outlined the procedures for a "controlled landing." To crash it so it could be stripped for whatever metal could be of use. Her first duty was to send the Dáinn on its last.

Ionie tracked how a cargo hauler banked away from the cargo bay. It loaded the atmospheric processors created for the Plateau below. A fuel-skimmer loomed in the darkness like a bloated tick. Outside, the Bygul and the Trjegul drifted in parallel orbits. They would share Dáinn's fate.

[#tack]Tack. Pause. [#crrrk]Crrrk. Tack. Heat drove through the circulation lines, drawing a low creak from the bulkheads. To the guardians, it was merely the groan of a dying vessel settling into its grave. To Ionie, it was an opening chord. Ionie had trained her ear for it. She applied a subtle pressure to the docked slate, triggering a micro-sensor calibrated for structural resonance. It drank the vibrations traveling through the floor bolts and up the spine of the captain's chair.
The lyrics. That was how Dom had named his hack. With a little help from Steven Gail, they'd worked it into a clandestine telegraph.
A single line of gray text bled across the dark glass, barely visible:
[ LUNGS BYPASSED ]

It blipped three times then disappeared. The docked cargo hauler's crew believed they were vacuuming the Dáinn's oxygen reserves, but Gail had rerouted the intakes. The pumps instead pull a pressurized slurry of inert nitrogen—a waste ballast.

Ionie took a sip from Ben's old mug. She had to force herself to gulp down the bitter drab, blinking hard. The wooden mask of the figurehead shattered. Salt-crust flakes burst from her skin. She shook her head to shake the dream.
Tack. Pause. Crrrk. Tack.
[MESS]
Ionie nudged the mug. The [#mug-shatter]ceramic shattered across the deck, coffee spreading in a dark fan between the captain's chair and the bridge rail.

paragraphs.text: "[steady]Damn it," she said, already rising. "[steady]I'll have it cleaned."
paragraphs.speaker: ionie-jia

paragraphs: She bent, picked up the largest shards, and straightened. One of the guardians moved at once, boots shifting, but the other remained fixed behind the chair.

paragraphs.text: "[steady]I don't need assistance," Ionie added, not looking back. "[steady]I'll be gone in a pulse."
paragraphs.speaker: ionie-jia

paragraphs: A half-second pause. Yet the guardian still fell in beside her as she left the bridge. Panels bore scuffs that no one had bothered to buff out. Bulkheads sweated faintly where temperature gradients failed to agree. A garbage shaft stood open. Someone had taped a handwritten notice over it: TEMPORARILY OUT OF ORDER. She eyeballed the shards. Then the guardian. One of his hands rested near a weapon in his belt without committing to it.

paragraphs.text: "[even]Real ceramic," she said, tone even.
paragraphs.speaker: ionie-jia

paragraphs: The guard nodded—just enough—and stepped aside to clear her path. The guardian kept half a step behind her as she proceeded toward the mess hall. They passed a junction where gravity shifted by a fraction. The floor dipped. Further in, the air changed again. Warmer. Wetter. The mess announced itself before the signage did. Yeast, spice. The door [#door-slide]slid open. Behind her, the guardian stopped at the threshold, posture settling into watch.
Omar stood behind the counter with his sleeves rolled up, forearms dusted in flour. A pot simmered beside him. He glanced up as Ionie entered.

paragraphs.text: "[warm]Captain," he said, as if the title still meant something practical. "[wry]You're taking the ship apart already?"
paragraphs.speaker: omar-newton

paragraphs: She almost laughed.

paragraphs.text: "[dry]I broke a mug."
paragraphs.speaker: ionie-jia

paragraphs.text: Omar clicked his tongue, already reaching for another. "[low]Ben's?"
paragraphs.speaker: omar-newton

paragraphs: She nodded.

paragraphs.text: "[warm]That one had opinions," he said. "[warm]Shame."
paragraphs.speaker: omar-newton

paragraphs: He slid a replacement across the counter. Ionie took it. Omar leaned closer, lowering his voice without looking at the guard.

paragraphs.text: "[low]Martel's cooked something."
paragraphs.speaker: omar-newton

paragraphs: Ionie leaned on the counter, her hands sliding over the mosaic tiles. With a wink, he passed her a lunchbox. She opened it. A sandwich cut cleanly in half. She lifted the liner underneath, tucked against the false bottom. A compartment slid free. Inside lay a wafer-thin slate. It displayed cryopod biotelemetry from the COG network. Pre-transit baseline. Martel's annotation surfaced alongside the stream.
This is not the record of a human entering the Fold for the first time. This is a record of someone who has been through it before.
Ionie scrolled.

I pulled this information from our improvised EGG. We've connected it to the COG network. Kaspar Savannen erased this and stored a backup in this drive. The biotelemetry belongs to an individual named "Ian Perera".
The clandestine archives. The early expeditions into the Yreus system. Pioneers dispatched before the fleet. This individual had been one of them.
Her nails bit into the soft composite of the lunchbox. She swallowed, returned the sandwich, and closed the box.

paragraphs.text: "[wry]That's the look of someone who's lost their appetite," Omar said. "[wry]I'll save it for later."
paragraphs.speaker: omar-newton
